title: Intel 8085 Microprocessor Architecture
examTip: 8085 is 8-bit, 16-bit address bus (64 KB max). Pins AD0–AD7 are multiplexed — ALE signal separates address from data. Five flags: S (Sign), Z (Zero), AC (Auxiliary Carry), P (Parity), CY (Carry). Five interrupts: TRAP > RST7.5 > RST6.5 > RST5.5 > INTR.

children.label: ALU  (8-bit Arithmetic & Logic Unit)
sublabels: ADD  SUB  ANA  ORA  XRA  CMP  RLC  RRC  RAL  RAR

children.label: Flag Register
sublabels: S (Sign)   Z (Zero)   AC (Aux Carry)   P (Parity)   CY (Carry)

children.label: General Purpose Registers
sublabels: H  |  L   (HL pair — most-used memory pointer)

children.label: Timing & Control Unit
sublabels: ALE  IO/M  RD  WR  READY  HOLD  HLDA  RESET OUT

children.label: Address Buffer / Latch
sublabels: AD0–AD7: multiplexed — ALE=1: Address, ALE=0: Data

buses.label: A8–A15  Upper 8-bit Address Bus (Dedicated — always address)
AD0–AD7  Multiplexed Lower Address / Data Bus  (ALE demultiplexes)
Control Bus:  ALE  IO/M  RD  WR  READY  HOLD  HLDA  RESET  CLK

theory:
The Intel 8085 is an enhanced 8-bit microprocessor introduced in 1977, a successor to the 8080. It operates from a single +5V supply with a built-in clock generator and requires fewer support chips, making it ideal for embedded applications. It has a 16-bit address bus (A0–A15) addressing 64 KB of memory and an 8-bit data bus.

ALU and Accumulator: The 8-bit ALU performs arithmetic (ADD, SUB, INR, DCR) and logical (ANA, ORA, XRA, CMA) operations. The Accumulator (register A) is the primary operand and result register for all ALU operations. Five flags reflect the ALU result: Sign (S) — set if result MSB=1; Zero (Z) — set if result=0; Auxiliary Carry (AC) — carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD arithmetic); Parity (P) — set if result has even number of 1s; Carry (CY) — carry out of MSB.

Register Array: Six 8-bit general-purpose registers B, C, D, E, H, L are organized in 16-bit pairs (BC, DE, HL) for 16-bit memory operations. HL is the most frequently used memory pointer. Stack Pointer (SP) is a 16-bit register pointing to the top of the LIFO stack. Program Counter (PC) is a 16-bit register holding the address of the next instruction to execute.

Control Unit: The Instruction Register (IR) captures the opcode from the data bus. The Instruction Decoder generates appropriate internal signals. The Timing & Control Unit generates bus control signals: ALE (Address Latch Enable — pulses high during T1 to indicate AD0–AD7 carry address), IO/M (distinguishes memory from I/O operations), RD, WR.

Multiplexed Bus: Pins AD0–AD7 are multiplexed — during T1 state, they carry the lower 8 bits of address; during T2/T3 states, they carry data. An external 8-bit latch (e.g., 74LS373) uses the ALE signal to demultiplex the bus.

Interrupts: 8085 supports 5 hardware interrupts in descending priority: TRAP (non-maskable, highest), RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and INTR (maskable, lowest). INTA is the interrupt acknowledge signal.
keyPoints: 8085 is 8-bit data bus, 16-bit address bus → addresses 64 KB (2^16) of memory
AD0–AD7 pins are multiplexed: carry address during T1 (ALE=1) and data during T2/T3
Five flags: S (Sign), Z (Zero), AC (Auxiliary Carry), P (Parity), CY (Carry)
Five hardware interrupts in order of priority: TRAP > RST7.5 > RST6.5 > RST5.5 > INTR
HL register pair is the most important — used as a 16-bit memory data pointer
useCases: Embedded control systems: traffic lights, home automation, vending machines
Industrial automation: process controllers, programmable logic controllers
Educational lab kits (8085 trainer kits) for microprocessor course practicals
